tourS1Desc: Your new intelligence center for detecting financial anomalies. Let's take a quick tour.
tourS2Desc: Upload your spreadsheets (CSV, Excel) here. The system will automatically load the transaction database.
tourS3Desc: Choose the mathematical method (Sigma, Z-Score) and adjust the AI sensitivity to find suspicious transactions.
tourS4Desc: Your database will appear in this table. Just click 'Analyze' to generate charts and identify fraud.

tourS7Desc: You might already know this, but here you can change the system language at any time.
tourS8Desc: Prefer working in the dark? Toggle between Light and Dark themes right here.
tourS9Desc: Finally, here you can view your details and log out. Enjoy the system!

errLgpd: You must accept the Privacy Terms to create an account.
termsContent:
PRIVACY AND DATA PROCESSING TERMS

1. Data Collection: We collect only the information strictly necessary for account creation.
2. Purpose: Your data will be used exclusively for authentication, access control, and age validation in the DataGuard system.
3. File Processing: Financial data and spreadsheets sent for analysis are processed in memory and/or temporarily stored confidentially, and are not used for other purposes.
4. Your Rights: In accordance with data protection laws, you may request the deletion of your account and all your data at any time.

* This is an academic project for non-commercial purposes.
infoMinRows: Minimum required: The file must contain at least 20 rows (transactions) for statistical calculation.
sysInfoText:
1. What is the system

DataGuard is a suspicious transaction detection system. It receives a spreadsheet or CSV file, analyzes the values of a numeric column, and identifies which records are outside the expected pattern. The system allows you to upload files, configure the analysis, view suspects in a table and chart, and download the results.

2. How to use the system

The operation is simple:

  1. Log into the system.
  2. Upload a .csv, .xlsx, or .xls file.
  3. Define the analysis settings.
  4. Choose the saved database and click Analyze.
  5. View the result in cards, table, and chart.
  6. Download the table or chart, if you want.

3. Important file rule

The system states that the file must have at least 20 rows for the statistical analysis to be appropriate. This appears directly on the interface as a minimum requirement.

Why is this important?

Because with little data, the analysis becomes weak and less reliable. The more consistent the database, the better the result tends to be.

4. What each field means

Database name

It is just a name to identify the file within the system. Example: “Week 2 Payments”.

File

This is the spreadsheet that will be sent. The system accepts: CSV, XLSX, XLS.

Column

This is the name of the column that will be analyzed. The system default is value. If your spreadsheet uses another name, like price, payment, or total, the user needs to change this field.

Direction

Defines where the system should look for anomalies:

  • Only above: looks for very high values
  • Only below: looks for very low values
  • Both: looks on both sides
Max. suspects

Defines the limit of suspicious records that will be displayed in the response. Example: if there are 2,000 suspects and the limit is 500, the system shows 500, but still knows it found 2,000.

Batch reading

This is an option for large CSV files. It helps to analyze the file in parts, without heavily burdening the memory. This option only applies to Sigma and Z-score.

5. What 'k' means

The k is the value that controls the sensitivity of the analysis.

  • Smaller k = more sensitive analysis
  • Larger k = more strict analysis
Example:
  • k = 1.5: finds more suspects
  • k = 3: balance
  • k = 4 or 5: finds fewer suspects

So, the k works as a fine-tuning adjustment. It tells how far a value needs to deviate from the pattern to be considered suspicious.

6. Types of methods

Sigma

Uses the mean and standard deviation to create a range considered normal. Values far outside this range are marked as suspicious.
In short: Good for detecting values that deviate greatly from the mean.

Z-score

Also uses mean and standard deviation, but measures how far each value is from the mean in statistical terms.
In short: It is a classic way to measure the deviation of each value.

IQR

Uses the quartiles of the database to find values outside the central region of the data.
In short: It is useful when there are extreme values and you want an analysis more resistant to distortions.

MAD

Uses the median instead of the mean, along with the dispersion based on it.
In short: It is a more robust method, very good for databases with outliers or heavily distorted values.

7. How the system identifies suspects

After the user chooses the method, the system:

  1. Reads the spreadsheet;
  2. Converts the chosen column to a number;
  3. Calculates the statistical limits;
  4. Compares each value with these limits;
  5. Separates the suspicious records.

If the value falls outside the defined range, it is marked as a suspect.

8. What appears in the result

After the analysis, the system shows:

  • Method used
  • Calculated statistics
  • Analysis limits
  • Number of suspects
  • Table with suspicious records
  • Chart with normal and suspicious points

This helps the user understand not only how many suspects exist, but also why they were marked.

9. Chart

The chart shows the behavior of the analyzed values and highlights the suspicious points. Normal values appear as a general trend, and suspects are visually highlighted.

This makes interpretation much easier, because the user can quickly see where the deviations are.

10. Precautions when using the system

For the analysis to work well, the user must:

  • Upload a valid file;
  • Ensure the spreadsheet has at least 20 rows;
  • Check if the reported column actually exists;
  • Use consistent numerical values;
  • Choose a method suitable for the database type.

The system also tries to interpret numbers in common formats, including values with currency symbols, decimal commas, and other spreadsheet formats.

11. Final summary

DataGuard works like this: the user uploads a database, chooses how to analyze the data, and the system identifies which records deviate from the statistical pattern.

The most important points to understand are:

  • The database must have at least 20 rows;
  • The default column is value;
  • The k defines the sensitivity;
  • The methods change how suspicious values are calculated;
  • The result appears in cards, table, and chart.
